summary: The post systematically debunks several widely-cited scientific findings by actually reading the original papers, showing that popular interpretations of studies on Dunning-Kruger effect, income and happiness, hedonic adaptation, and chess expertise are often opposite to what the research actually found. Luu argues that reading academic papers is much easier than people think - often requiring just reading the abstract, conclusion, and skimming graphs to verify claims. The post emphasizes that gatekeeping around scientific literacy is often self-serving, as the knowledge required to interpret most studies can be acquired in far less time than a full degree program.
key_points: Dunning-Kruger is commonly misinterpreted - the study shows perceived ability is compressed (not inverted), with people at both low and high skill levels misestimating their abilities
Income consistently correlates with happiness across all countries studied, contrary to popular claims about happiness caps at $75k or lower
Hedonic adaptation theory is wrong - people don't fully return to baseline happiness after negative events like unemployment, disability, or divorce
Chess expertise advantage persists even with randomized positions (1.6x vs 2.2x for real positions), contradicting the widely-cited claim that the advantage completely disappears
Reading empirical papers to verify claims is usually straightforward - reading abstracts, conclusions, and skimming graphs is often sufficient to catch misrepresentations
money_quotes: It's really common to see claims that some meme is backed by 'studies' or 'science'. But when I look at the actual studies, it usually turns out that the data are opposed to the claim.
Reading papers sounds like a lot of work. It sometimes is. But it's often not.

Gatekeeping reading and interpreting a study on whether or not someone has a credential like a degree is absurd when someone can spend a week's worth of time gaining the knowledge that a degree offers.
It's in the best interests of non-altruistic people who do read studies to make it seem like reading studies is difficult.
People love clever, contrarian, ideas and don't care very much if they're wrong, so clever, contrarian [ideas] relatively frequently become viral relative to their correctness.
key_insight: Most popular interpretations of scientific studies are wrong because people don't actually read the papers, but verifying claims by reading the original research is far easier than the gatekeepers of scientific literacy would have you believe.
